chunks.title: Introduction to the Diabetes Prevention Program Trial
chunks.summary: The segment introduces the Diabetes Prevention Program trial, highlighting its significance in managing diabetes risks through lifestyle changes. The speaker notes that the trial demonstrated a lifestyle intervention could be more effective than medication in preventing diabetes among participants.
chunks.transcript: but there's something kind of like that and you talk about this in the book The diabetes prevention program trial right you want to talk about that one absolutely this was published in the New England Journal to talk about something that the New England Journal did well um this was published in the New England Journal in 2002 and it was exactly that study it was taking people at high risk for diabetes and making three arms not two there's not just a drug arm and a placebo they added a lifestyle alarm and this is a really important study because docs are taught subliminally that there's no sense in wasting time trying to help people to change their lifestyle because they won't do it don't waste your time just go ahead and treat them with whatever you're going to treat them with well this study showed that the people who took metformin the the rightful first-line drug to treat type 2 diabetes

chunks.title: The Impact of Medication vs. Lifestyle Change
chunks.summary: This section contrasts metformin, a first-line diabetes medication, with lifestyle modifications. The speaker argues that while metformin reduced diabetes incidence, lifestyle changes were even more effective, showing a significant 58 fewer cases of diabetes in the lifestyle group compared to placebo.
chunks.transcript: um developed uh 31 uh less diabetes than the people who took Placebo so that's a good thing in my heart of hearts this is not um this is now my opinion everything I've said so far is fact this is my opinion I think it's just artificially suppressing the blood sugar I don't think it's undoing the process that's diabetes that's really responsible for the heart disease and the amputations and the baby failure or not right but in any event 31 fewer people got diagnosed with diabetes when they were treated with metformin compared to the people who were treated with Placebo for the people in the lifestyle group 58 fewer people develop diabetes than the people who were treated with Placebo and that's for real and those people even though the program lasted I forget six months or a year or something those people kept their benefits over 10 years they kept their weight loss they continued to exercise and it's just a pure

chunks.title: Critique of Medical Education on Lifestyle Interventions
chunks.summary: The speaker critiques medical education for failing to emphasize lifestyle interventions in treating chronic illnesses. They share personal experiences and insights from other physicians about the lack of training in recommending and supporting lifestyle changes for patients.
chunks.transcript: demonstration of how the corporate America is determining what what doctors are the the so-called knowledge the quote knowledge that doctors are getting that uh causes them to think that they're practicing medicine the right way when they're being distracted from practicing medicine the right way it's crazy I was not taught to think about lifestyle interventions in medical school and I went to the University of Arizona there's better medical schools maybe maybe in a better medical school but I doubt it better medical schools probably teach it less I don't they're more into the uh you know the reductionist uh science and that's my impression I mean I'm just trying to you know be you know I I had you know my experience in PA school and my experience in medical school and but other doctors I've spoken to are the same way you know my father's a physician he went to Georgetown you know decades before I went to medical school and and I I guess I've known Physicians who went to all sorts of medical schools and and consistently I do not hear them learning about lifestyle interventions and my father was an internist and he even told me growing up I remember him telling me patients won't do it patients won't do the lifestyle changes and and I thought oh patients won't do lifestyle changes my dad's trying and I think it's probably because we're not telling them the way to do it or we don't have a system that's set up to give them the support that they need to make the lifestyle changes but the diabetes prevention program trial is so striking the fact that you can give them one of the first line drugs that's very

chunks.title: Need for Comprehensive Lifestyle Studies
chunks.summary: In this segment, the speaker advocates for more comprehensive lifestyle studies in clinical trials, especially those concerning lifestyle-related diseases. They question why lifestyle interventions are often omitted from drug studies, implying a conflict of interest with the pharmaceutical industry.
chunks.transcript: effective for diabetes Metformin and find that lifestyle intervention is better why don't we have a trial like you said paid for by Pfizer or AstraZeneca or whatever Statin manufacturer wants to manufacture their Statin which does Mediterranean diet whatever diet you want Mediterranean diet because we have Diet Leon study I would prefer something a little different than a Mediterranean diet but Mediterranean diet versus you know Lipitor atorvastatin reservastatin versus control that would be a cool trial yeah or why do I want to go a step further why not in a medical condition that is clearly related to Lifestyle nobody would deny that why not mandate that any study of a drug that is supposed to reduce the risk of heart disease has to have a lifestyle study yeah a lifestyle there's it's no sense because you're not the doctors think and the patients think that these studies are determining the best way to prevent heart disease and they're not they don't have a lifestyle on so why does the uh FDA allow these trials to keep going on

chunks.title: Details of the Lifestyle Modification Program
chunks.summary: The details of the lifestyle modification program from the Diabetes Prevention Program trial are discussed, emphasizing goals like weight loss and regular physical activity. This highlights the specific targets set for participants to achieve better health outcomes.
chunks.transcript: without a lifestyle alarm and that's because of the influence of the pharmaceutical industry on the fdm I mean the FDA has funded the division of the FDA that addresses human products 61 percent of its funding comes from the drug companies I just want to read from the diabetes prevention program trial and so it was 300 3234 non-diabetic persons they had elevated fasting and post load plasma glucose concentrations and they were randomized to Placebo metformin and it was 850 milligrams twice daily or a lifestyle modification program and this is what I wanted to highlight and the goals of the lifestyle modification program were at least a seven percent weight loss and 150 minutes of physical activity per week so that was that was what they did and you can read the trial if you want it's easily available online and you can you can see what else they did but that was their goal was 150

chunks.title: Broader Implications of Lifestyle on Health
chunks.summary: The conversation expands to discuss how many diseases, including cancer and cardiovascular issues, are linked to lifestyle choices. The speaker suggests that chronic illnesses often have lifestyle-related components, reinforcing the need for well-structured preventative programs.
chunks.transcript: minutes of exercise per week they wanted people to lose seven um seven percent weight loss so they want people to lose weight and exercise and I imagine there were some dietary recommendations which were probably at the level of stop eating junk food and processed sugars they're probably and that that level of intervention was significantly more effective than metformin which is which is striking and I agree with you completely every I think every trial which we know is in a lifestyle disease and I think so many diseases are lifestyle related even the ones we don't think of as lifestyle diseases including cancer including cancer exactly you'd have to do a long-term study about prevention that would take decades and denture cancer certainly cardiovascular disease certainly diabetes certainly anything with obesity hypertension the list is long of of you know conditions that are considered chronic and are often considered idiopathic uh primary hypertension that certainly have lifestyle uh connections and they I believe there should be a third arm that
